text: Thank you for the moderator. Good afternoon, everyone. I'm Dr. Li from Guangzhou, China. My topic is a novel technique for laparoscopic right hemicolectomy with CME, the caudal two cranial mites approach.
The complete CME procedure can include sub-dissection of visceral and parietal fascia, and ligation at the root of the central vessels, and radical lymph dissection.

There are three main approaches regarding laparoscopic red colectomy. That is the lateral-to-medial approach, and the medial-to-lateral approach, and the cranial-to-causal approach. Now we introduce a new one. That is the outer-to-cranial approach.

The surgeon confirms the presence of the tumor and moves the small intestine and omentum to the left upper quadrant. The appendix and last ileal loop are dressed using syncytophoresis. The mesentery root is exposed and placed under tension for sub-dissection at its caudal aspect. Access is gained to the vascular plane between the geotracheal and visceral fissures of the mesocolon. Dissection proceeds laterally, medially, and cephalically, separating the ascending colon from retroperitoneal tissues, the duodenum, and the pancreatic head. The dissection continues to the lateral attachment of the right colon, exposing the SMA medially and identifying the pancreatic head cephalically. The duodenum and SMA are visible posteriorly, and gauze is placed for indication after dissection.
The surgeon performs blind dissection followed by sub-dissection, progressing to lymph node dissection and division of vessels within the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the ileocolic vessels and SMOE, incising the peritoneum along these vessels. The incision extends into the previously dissected right peritoneal space. Lymph nodes surrounding the ileocolic vessels are dissected, and the root of the ileocolic vessels is identified, cut, and ligated.

The surgeon divides the ileocolic vessels, including the vein and artery, by ligating and cutting them. The dissection proceeds from caudal to cranial direction. The right colic vessels are typically present, but are absent in this patient. The gastrocolic vessels are carefully identified, though the superior right colic vessel is also absent. The middle colic vessels are addressed next, with the right branches being cut. For an extended right colectomy, the middle colic vessels are ligated and cut at the root, allowing the dissection to continue until the posterior wall of the stomach is visible.

The surgeon dissects the gastrocolic ligament along the greater curvature of the stomach. The transverse mesocolon is dissected, and the hepatic flexure is mobilized. The dissection of the mesocolon continues downwards.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the last lateral attachment of the right colon, fully freeing the entire right colon. The visual shows the different vessels exposed after dissection. Subsequently, a side-to-side anastomosis is performed extracorporeally.
